source: （中英）润色后-明十三陵导览词.docx
paragraphs: 明十三陵 / The Ming Tombs
开场白 / Intro
各位朋友，咱们现在走进的这片被天寿山环抱、松柏掩映的区域，就是明朝13位皇帝的长眠之地——北京明十三陵！咱们顺着"神道→长陵→定陵"的经典路线逛。出发！

Hello everyone, and welcome! I'm Miya, your guide today. We're now entering a valley cradled by Tianshou Mountain and shaded by towering pines and cypresses. This is the Ming Tombs—the final resting place of 13 emperors of the Ming Dynasty. Today we'll follow the classic route: first the Divine Way, the sacred avenue that connects all the tombs; then Changling, the magnificent mausoleum of Emperor Yongle, home to the largest surviving wooden hall in China; and finally Dingling, where we'll descend into the only underground palace in the complex that's been opened to the public. Let's get going!
一、概况与历史沿革 / I. Overview & Historical Evolution
1. 位置与布局 / Location & Layout
明十三陵位于北京昌平区天寿山南麓，占地约120平方公里，遵循"风水宝地"原则，13座陵寝以长陵为中心环绕分布。

The Ming Tombs sit at the southern foot of Tianshou Mountain in Changping District, covering about 120 square kilometers. The layout follows classical feng shui principles to the letter: the complex backs onto the mountain range and faces a gentle river, with all 13 mausoleums arranged around Changling—the tomb of Emperor Yongle—like stars orbiting the moon. A grand avenue called the Divine Way, flanked by stone statues of officials and animals, serves as the ceremonial approach to the entire site.
2. 十三座陵寝名称 / Names of the 13 Mausoleums
长陵（永乐）、献陵（洪熙）、景陵（宣德）、裕陵（正统/天顺）、茂陵（成化）、泰陵（弘治）、康陵（正德）、永陵（嘉靖）、昭陵（隆庆）、定陵（万历）、庆陵（泰昌）、德陵（天启）、思陵（崇祯）。

In chronological order: Changling (Emperor Yongle), Xianling (Hongxi), Jingling (Xuande), Yuling (Zhengtong/Tianshun), Maoling (Chenghua), Tailing (Hongzhi), Kangling (Zhengde), Yongling (Jiajing), Zhaoling (Longqing), Dingling (Wanli), Qingling (Taichang), Deling (Tianqi), and Siling (Chongzhen). Changling is by far the largest and most grand. Dingling holds a different kind of distinction—it's the only tomb that's been excavated and opened to visitors.
3. 明代帝陵规制 / Ming Imperial Mausoleum Norms
明代帝陵都遵循"前方后圆"布局：前半方形祭祀区，后半圆形宝城。象征"天圆地方"。

Every Ming imperial tomb shares the same fundamental layout: "square in front, round in back." The front section is a rectangular courtyard used for sacrificial ceremonies, containing the tomb gate, the Gate of Eminent Favor, and the Hall of Eminent Favor. The rear section is a circular walled enclosure called the Treasure City, beneath which lies the underground burial chamber. This design reflects the ancient Chinese cosmological principle of "round heaven and square earth."
4. 未葬入十三陵的皇帝 / Emperors Not Buried Here
朱元璋葬于南京明孝陵；建文帝下落不明；崇祯葬于核心区外的思陵。

The Ming Dynasty had 16 emperors in total, but only 13 are buried here. The founding emperor, Zhu Yuanzhang, established his capital in Nanjing and was laid to rest in the Ming Xiaoling Mausoleum there. Emperor Jianwen simply vanished after being overthrown in the Jingnan Campaign—no tomb, no remains. And Emperor Chongzhen, the last Ming emperor, hanged himself when the dynasty fell; his ministers buried him in a small tomb called Siling, located outside the main cemetery.
5. 长陵选址与营造 / Changling Site Selection
永乐皇帝选定天寿山有三大原因：风水绝佳、迁都需求、彰显皇权。1409年动工，选用金丝楠木。

Changling is Emperor Yongle's tomb, and it's the anchor of the entire complex. He chose Tianshou Mountain for three reasons. First, the feng shui is exceptional—the site has mountains at the back, water in front, and excellent energy flow, meeting every criterion in the imperial manual. Second, after moving the capital to Beijing in 1421, he needed a permanent royal cemetery for himself and his successors. Third, and perhaps most importantly, the construction was meant to project imperial power. Work began in 1409, using the finest golden nanmu timber hauled from the deep mountains of Yunnan and Sichuan, with hundreds of thousands of craftsmen laboring for years.
二、长陵 / II. Changling Tomb
1. 永乐皇帝生平 / Emperor Yongle
永乐皇帝在位22年，完成三件大事：发动靖难之役、迁都北京、修建长陵。

Emperor Yongle—born Zhu Di—was the third emperor of the Ming Dynasty and ruled for 22 years. He was a larger-than-life figure who pulled off three transformative achievements: he seized the throne through the Jingnan Campaign, moved the capital to Beijing and oversaw the construction of the Forbidden City, and established Changling along with the complete set of norms that all subsequent Ming tombs would follow.
2. 中轴线建筑 / Central Axis Buildings
从外到内：陵门、祾恩门、祾恩殿、内红门、明楼、宝城。

Walking along Changling's central axis, you pass through six structures in sequence: the Tomb Gate (main entrance, with "Changling" inscribed by Emperor Qianlong), the Gate of Eminent Favor (second courtyard gate), the Hall of Eminent Favor (the core ceremonial hall), the Inner Red Gate (marking the boundary between the sacrificial and burial areas), the Ming Tower (a pavilion housing a stone stele), and finally the Treasure City (the circular walled mound beneath which the unexcavated underground palace lies).
3. 祾恩殿 / Hall of Eminent Favor
祾恩殿高29米、面宽9间，中国现存最大木构大殿。60根金丝楠木柱，最粗直径1.17米，全靠榫卯拼接。

Look up. This is the Hall of Eminent Favor—29 meters tall, 9 bays wide, and the largest surviving wooden structure in China. The magic is in the materials and engineering. Inside stand 60 massive pillars of golden nanmu. The thickest one measures 1.17 meters in diameter—you'd need two adults to wrap their arms around it. These logs came from remote forests in Yunnan, and legend has it that transporting a single pillar took thousands of laborers, hundreds of mules and horses, and nearly three years. The entire hall was assembled using traditional mortise-and-tenon joinery—no iron nails at all—and it has stood firm for over 600 years.
三、定陵 / III. Dingling Tomb
1. 墓主人与地面建筑 / Tomb Owner
定陵是万历皇帝的陵寝，在位48年，明朝最久。重点参观地下27米处的地宫。

Dingling is the tomb of Emperor Wanli, the 13th Ming emperor, who held the throne for 48 years—the longest reign in the dynasty. His two empresses are buried here as well. By the time archaeologists arrived in the 1950s, most of the above-ground structures had been reduced to ruins by centuries of war and neglect. The real treasure lies 27 meters underground.
2. 发掘过程 / Excavation
1956年启动发掘，找到金刚墙。地宫封闭200多年，严重缺氧。1959年开放。因教训惨痛，国家后来禁止主动发掘帝王陵。

The excavation officially began in 1956 and was anything but easy. The team first had to locate the entrance—they spotted an arched opening in the Treasure City wall and dug downward until they hit the Diamond Wall, the massive stone barrier sealing the underground palace. Inside, the air had been stagnant for over 200 years, so workers had to carry oxygen tanks. Many artifacts, especially silk fabrics, deteriorated the moment they were exposed to fresh air. The underground palace opened to visitors in 1959. The experience was so sobering that the Chinese government enacted a firm policy: no more active excavation of imperial tombs.
3. 地宫布局 / Underground Palace Layout
地宫1195平方米，分五殿：前殿、中殿（三座宝座和长明灯）、后殿（万历棺椁及26箱珍宝）、左右配殿（空置）。

The underground palace covers 1,195 square meters and is divided into five chambers, laid out like a miniature subterranean palace. The Front Hall is a bare entry corridor. The Middle Hall is the ceremonial heart, with three white marble thrones for the emperor and his two empresses, each with a glazed lampstand in front—the so-called "ever-burning lamps," though by the time they were opened, the oil had long since solidified. The Rear Hall is the main burial chamber: Emperor Wanli's coffin sits in the center, flanked by those of his empresses, surrounded by 26 red-lacquered wooden boxes filled with gold, silver, jade, silk, and other treasures. The two Side Halls were prepared for concubines who were never actually buried there, so they remain empty.
4. 金井玉葬 / Burial with Jade in the Golden Well
棺床正中的"金井"直径30厘米，内藏万历玉器。"金井玉葬"寓意接地气、灵魂不朽。

Notice the small pit in the center of the stone coffin platform in the Rear Hall—that's the "Golden Well," only about 30 centimeters across. Inside it, archaeologists found jade belts, pendants, and other jade articles belonging to Emperor Wanli. This was part of an exclusive Ming Dynasty ritual called "Burial with Jade in the Golden Well." The well symbolized a connection to the earth's vital energy, while jade represented eternity and immortality. Together, the ritual expressed the hope that the emperor would rest in peace and that his spirit would endure forever.
四、世界荣誉 / IV. World Honor
2003年，明十三陵被列入《世界遗产名录》。

In 2003, the Ming Tombs—along with the Ming Xiaoling Mausoleum in Nanjing—were jointly inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, recognized as outstanding representatives of Ming imperial funerary architecture.
欢送词 / Farewell Speech
各位朋友，明十三陵之旅到这里就圆满结束啦！希望今天的讲解能让大家记住长陵的木构奇迹、定陵的地宫秘密。祝大家旅途愉快，再见！

Well everyone, that wraps up our Ming Tombs tour! We've walked the sacred avenue of the Divine Way, marveled at the nanmu pillars of Changling, and descended into the underground palace of Dingling. This land, watched over by Tianshou Mountain, carries the weight of 276 years of Ming Dynasty history. I hope today gave you a real sense of the grandeur and the stories hidden here. Thanks for a great day, and safe travels!
